mnemonics.title: RCD = Row-Column-Different
mnemonics.content: Three encryption rules: Row = shift Right, Column = shift Down, Different (rectangle) = swap columns. For decryption: Row = Left, Column = Up, Different = same swap.

mnemonics.title: 5x5 = 25 = No J
mnemonics.content: 5x5 grid holds 25 letters. 26 minus 1 = I and J share a cell. Keyword fills first (no repeats), then remaining alphabet letters in order.

mnemonics.title: XX Marks the Split
mnemonics.content: Double letters get split by X: BALLOON becomes BA-LX-LO-ON. Odd-length plaintext gets padded with X at the end. X is the universal filler.

mnemonics.title: 676 Beats 26
mnemonics.content: Monoalphabetic: 26 single-letter substitutions. Playfair: 26x26 = 676 digraph combinations. More possibilities = flatter frequency distribution = harder to break.

mnemonics.title: Wheatstone Made It, Playfair Named It
mnemonics.content: Charles Wheatstone invented it (1854). Lord Playfair promoted it. The British military used it in the Boer War and WWI. Named after the promoter, not the inventor.

keyPoints: The Playfair cipher encrypts digraphs (pairs of letters) using a 5x5 key matrix where I and J share a cell.
Three rules based on letter positions: same row (shift right), same column (shift down), rectangle (swap columns).
Plaintext preparation: insert 'X' between double letters, pad with 'X' if odd length, replace J with I.
More secure than monoalphabetic ciphers due to 676 digraph combinations vs. 26 single-letter substitutions.
Still breakable by digraph frequency analysis with sufficient ciphertext.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to replace J with I before building the key matrix or encrypting.
Failing to insert 'X' between repeated letters (e.g., encrypting 'LL' directly instead of 'LX' and 'L_').
Confusing decryption direction: same-row decryption shifts LEFT (not right), same-column shifts UP (not down).
Misapplying the rectangle rule: each letter stays in its OWN row but moves to the OTHER letter's column.
